questions.question: Explain the role of client and server stubs in RPC architecture.
expectedPoints: Client stub acts as proxy for remote procedure
Handles parameter marshalling and network communication
Server stub receives requests and makes local calls
Both stubs make remote calls appear local to programmers

questions.question: Compare and contrast the four RPC call semantics with appropriate examples.
expectedPoints: Maybe semantics: no execution guarantees
At-least-once: good for idempotent operations
At-most-once: essential for non-idempotent operations
Exactly-once: ideal but difficult to achieve in practice

questions.question: Describe the marshalling process in RPC with examples.
expectedPoints: Conversion of parameters to network format
Data representation standardization
Parameter ordering consistency
Serialization of complex data structures

questions.question: Explain the complete workflow of an RPC call from client initiation to result return.
expectedPoints: Client makes what appears to be local call
Client stub marshals parameters
Message sent over network to server
Server stub receives and unmarshals parameters
Server executes actual procedure
Results marshaled and returned to client
Client stub unmarshals results and returns to client program

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and limitations of RPC compared to other remote communication mechanisms.
expectedPoints: Advantage: programming model similar to local calls
Advantage: hides network complexity
Limitation: performance overhead compared to local calls
Limitation: more failure modes to handle
Comparison with message passing and socket programming
Suitability for different types of distributed applications

questions.question: Analyze the error handling challenges in RPC systems and possible solutions.
expectedPoints: Network failures: timeouts, packet loss
Server failures: crashes during execution
Parameter mismatches: type errors, version issues
Solutions: retry mechanisms, idempotent operations
Error reporting and exception handling strategies
Use of acknowledgments and sequence numbers
